title: practical english usage - Part 291
content:
Note the expressions any good/use and no good/use.

3   any/no       good;     any/no       use 'Is Jack any better? 'No different. Still very ill.' This school isn't any different from the last one.

We   can also use any and no with different.

2  any/no       different I'm afraid the weather's no better than yesterday.

No can also be used in this way (but not some).

ʃʃʃ     were any younger, I'd fall in love with you.

You don't look any older than your daughter. (= You don’ t look Can you go any faster?

sentences, and after (/(also ►    222).

Any can modify comparatives. This happens mostly in questions and negative 1  any     and   no   with    comparatives 373 any     and   no:   adverbs ►    550 another        and   other(s) ►    403 among       and   between They all went to the cinema together.

Come on, everybody sing. All together now. ..

All together usually means ‘ everybody/everything together'.

That's £4.38  altogether.

Altogether can also be used to give totals.

Altogether, she decided, marriage was a bit of a mistake.

My new house isn 't altogether finished.

Altogether means ‘ completely' or ‘ considering everything'.

372 altogether           and   all   together vocabulary' •  375  arise and rise For the difference between rise and raise, ►     1 .2.

(a)rise - (a)rose - (a)risen Arise and rise are irregular verbs.

with this meaning in a very formal style.

Note that we  usually say that people get up in the morning. Rise is only used My hopes are rising.

Prices keep rising. What time does the sun rise?

Rise usually means 'get higher', 'come/go up'.

I'm afraid a difficulty has arisen.

A discussion arose about the best way to pay.

with abstract nouns as subjects.

Arise means 'begin', 'appear', 'occur', 'come to one’ s notice’ . It is used mostly 375 arise      and   rise   __________ For structures with look, ►    518.

She suddenly appeared in the doorway.

modified by an adverbial.

Appear can also mean 'come into sight’  or 'arrive'. In this case it can be 3 'come    into    sight' hinrwulerstand.) but not I can't seem to make him  understand, (          I can't appear to m ake with appear.

Seem can be used in a special structure with can't (►   576.4). This is not possible It seemed like a good idea. (More natural than It appeared like a good idea.) Seem is often used with like. This is not normal with appear.

She doesn't want to go on studying. It seems a pity, (        appears a pity.) The baby seems/appears (to be) hungry.

about objective facts. Compare: impressions and feelings (►   576 for examples). Appear is mostly used to talk Seem can be used to talk both about objective facts and about subjective 2  appear        and   seem:     differences It appears that we may be mistaken.

There appears to be a problem with the oil pressure.

Structures with preparatory there (►   20) or it (►  268) are possible.

The boys on the bus appeared to be students.

It appears to be some kind of bomb.

Before nouns we  generally use appear to be.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  376   (a)round and about Since he had not paid his bill, his electricity was cut off.

As it's raining again, we'll have to stay at home.

often come at the beginning of sentences.

or when it is not the most important part of the sentence. As- and since-clauses As and since are used when the reason is already known to the listener/reader, 1 as   and   since since and for referring to time, ►    251, 469, 579.) There are some differences.

All four of these words can be used to refer to the reason for something. (For as, 377 as,   because,         since      and   for ►    515 as  and   like For other uses of these words, see a good dictionary.

* What time shall I come? ‘About/Around eight!

There were about/around fifty people there.

common than around in British English.

About and around can both mean ‘ approximately’ , ‘ not exactly'. About is more 4 approximately Stop fooling around/about. We're late.

wasting or silly activity.

We   also use these words in some common expressions to talk about time­ I like doing odd jobs around/about the house.
